content.text: The RAG Pattern in Enterprise Context

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) separates knowledge storage from language generation. Instead of baking domain knowledge into a fine-tuned model, RAG keeps that knowledge in a searchable index and injects the most relevant passages into the LLM's context window at query time. For enterprise systems this separation is critical: the knowledge base is owned, versioned, and access-controlled by the business, while the LLM can be swapped or upgraded without retraining.

A reference architecture is a reusable template that codifies decisions that should not be reinvented for each project. The enterprise RAG reference architecture below captures two pipelines — an offline indexing pipeline that keeps the knowledge base fresh, and an online query pipeline that serves answers — along with the governance hooks that make it auditable.

RAG is not a single component — it is a two-pipeline system. Getting either pipeline wrong degrades the other: stale or poorly chunked indexes make retrieval useless; poor retrieval makes even the best LLM hallucinate.

attrs.alt: RAG Reference Architecture: offline indexing pipeline (top) feeding the online query pipeline (bottom)

content.text: The indexing pipeline runs offline (typically on a scheduled trigger or on document change events) and populates the vector store that the query pipeline reads from. Each stage requires explicit decisions.
Enterprise document sources include SharePoint, Confluence, S3/GCS buckets, relational databases, and real-time event streams. Key design decisions:
Poll-based (scheduled full re-index) versus event-driven (webhook or change data capture). Event-driven gives fresher indexes but adds connector complexity.
ACLs defined at the source must be carried forward as metadata on each chunk. A chunk indexed without its ACL cannot be filtered during retrieval and will leak to unauthorized users.
PDFs with embedded images, scanned forms, and presentations require OCR before chunking. The indexing pipeline must know which content types require pre-processing.
Chunking controls the granularity of retrieval. Chunks that are too large fill the context window with irrelevant text; chunks that are too small lose surrounding context needed for coherent answers.
Sentences vary widely in information density
Slower to compute; requires embedding during indexing
Small child chunks indexed; parent chunk retrieved
More complex retrieval logic; larger storage
Custom per content type (table rows, code blocks)

For FNOL claims documentation, a hierarchical strategy works well: individual policy clause sentences are indexed as child chunks, while the full policy section is retrieved when any child matches. This preserves granular retrieval while surfacing enough context for the LLM to reason about the clause in context.
The embedding model converts text to dense vectors. The choice affects retrieval quality, cost, and latency:
General English; low cost; good default
Higher accuracy for long documents; 2× cost
Domain-specific with domain adaptation; strong multilingual
On-premise requirement; no external API call; multilingual

The enterprise standard embedding model should be captured in an ADR. Deviating requires an exception request — the embedding model couples the indexing and query pipelines: changing it requires re-indexing all documents.
Raw user queries are often ambiguous, use abbreviations, or lack context available in the conversation history. Query rewriting improves retrieval precision before any vector search occurs:

Ask the LLM to generate a hypothetical answer to the question, then embed that answer instead of the raw question. The hypothetical answer uses domain vocabulary, which tends to match indexed chunks better than a short question.
Expand acronyms and synonyms specific to the domain ("FNOL" → "first notice of loss", "MTPL" → "motor third party liability").
Generate 2–3 rephrased versions of the question, retrieve chunks for each, and union the results before re-ranking. Increases recall at the cost of more embedding and retrieval calls.

Dense retrieval (vector similarity) excels at semantic matching but misses exact keyword matches. Sparse retrieval (BM25) excels at exact term matching but misses paraphrases. Hybrid retrieval combines both signals:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Protocol

@dataclass
class Chunk:
    chunk_id: str
    text: str
    doc_id: str
    acl_roles: list[str]
    metadata: dict

class DenseRetriever(Protocol):
    def search(self, query_embedding: list[float], top_k: int, acl_filter: list[str]) -> list[tuple[Chunk, float]]: ...

class SparseRetriever(Protocol):
    def search(self, query_text: str, top_k: int, acl_filter: list[str]) -> list[tuple[Chunk, float]]: ...

def hybrid_retrieve(
    query_text: str,
    query_embedding: list[float],
    user_roles: list[str],
    dense: DenseRetriever,
    sparse: SparseRetriever,
    top_k: int = 20,
    alpha: float = 0.7,
) -> list[tuple[Chunk, float]]:
    # alpha = weight given to dense score (1 - alpha = sparse weight)
    dense_results = {c.chunk_id: (c, s) for c, s in dense.search(query_embedding, top_k * 2, user_roles)}
    sparse_results = {c.chunk_id: (c, s) for c, s in sparse.search(query_text, top_k * 2, user_roles)}

    all_ids = set(dense_results) | set(sparse_results)
    fused: list[tuple[Chunk, float]] = []
    for cid in all_ids:
        d_score = dense_results[cid][1] if cid in dense_results else 0.0
        s_score = sparse_results[cid][1] if cid in sparse_results else 0.0
        chunk = (dense_results.get(cid) or sparse_results[cid])[0]
        combined = alpha * d_score + (1 - alpha) * s_score
        fused.append((chunk, combined))

    fused.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    return fused[:top_k]

Every retrieval call must apply an access control filter before returning results. Failing to filter allows one user to receive chunks from documents they are not authorized to read — a serious data leak in multi-tenant enterprise systems.

ACL filtering must happen inside the vector store query, not as a post-retrieval client-side filter. Post-filtering reduces effective top-k and creates attack surfaces where a large enough top-k request bypasses access controls.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RetrievalRequest:
    query_embedding: list[float]
    query_text: str
    user_id: str
    user_roles: list[str]
    top_k: int = 10

def retrieve_with_acl(req: RetrievalRequest, vector_store) -> list[Chunk]:
    # ACL filter passed INTO the vector store query — not applied after
    results = vector_store.query(
        vector=req.query_embedding,
        filter={"acl_roles": {"$in": req.user_roles}},  # Pinecone metadata filter syntax
        top_k=req.top_k * 2,  # over-fetch to account for re-ranking
        include_metadata=True,
    )
    return [Chunk(
        chunk_id=r["id"],
        text=r["metadata"]["text"],
        doc_id=r["metadata"]["doc_id"],
        acl_roles=r["metadata"]["acl_roles"],
        metadata=r["metadata"],
    ) for r in results["matches"]]

The initial retrieval returns candidates ranked by embedding similarity. A cross-encoder re-ranker scores each candidate against the full query text, producing a more accurate relevance ranking. After re-ranking, context assembly packs the top chunks into the prompt context window:
Generation Layer: Prompt Structure and Guardrails

The generation prompt wraps the assembled context and user query in a structure that instructs the LLM to answer from the provided context only, cite sources, and decline when the context does not support a confident answer:

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are an enterprise knowledge assistant. Answer questions using ONLY the provided "
    "context passages. If the context does not contain sufficient information to answer "
    "confidently, say so explicitly rather than guessing. Cite the source document title "
    "and section for each factual claim using [Source: <title>] notation."
)

def build_generation_prompt(query: str, context: ContextWindow) -> list[dict]:
    context_text = "\n\n---\n\n".join(
        f"[Source: {c.metadata.get('title','Unknown')} | Section: {c.metadata.get('section','')}]\n{c.text}"
        for c in context.chunks
    )
    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Context:\n{context_text}\n\nQuestion: {query}"},
    ]
Enterprise guardrails wrap the generation call to intercept responses that contain PII, hallucinated citations, or content that violates policy. A guardrail layer checks:
Every [Source: X] reference in the response must map to a chunk in the context window. Fabricated citations are a hallucination signal.
Scan output for patterns matching SSN, account numbers, or personal identifiers before returning to the client.
If the LLM output contains hedge phrases ("I believe", "I think", "possibly") without a supporting source, flag for human review rather than surfacing the answer directly.
A RAG system has two quality dimensions that must be measured independently: retrieval quality and generation quality.
Fraction of relevant chunks appearing in top-k results
Offline eval on labelled query set
How highly the first relevant chunk is ranked
Fraction of retrieved chunks that actually help answer the query
Fraction of answer claims supported by retrieved context
Whether the answer addresses the user question

Every query-response pair should emit a structured trace event that records the query, retrieved chunks with scores, re-ranked order, prompt tokens, response, latency, and guardrail outcomes. This trace is the primary input for offline evaluation and supports audit requirements.
The reference architecture connects to several enterprise systems that are not part of the RAG pipeline itself but must be accounted for in the solution design:
Supply user roles to the ACL filter at query time
Token must be verified server-side; roles must not come from the client
Source of truth for enterprise knowledge; drives indexing triggers
Change notifications may be delayed; full re-index needed for ACL changes
LLM API keys, embedding model credentials, vector store access
Keys must rotate automatically; never committed to config files
Record every query, user, retrieved docs, and response for compliance
PII in query text may require masking before logging
Track per-project token spend for LLM and embedding API calls
Separate cost centres per department; alert on anomalous spend spikes
When instantiating this reference architecture for a project, the following decisions must be made and documented in the project's ADR set:
Which approved model; dimensions; whether EU data can be sent to the API or requires on-premise.
Managed SaaS (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud) versus self-hosted (pgvector, Qdrant). Drives latency, cost, and data residency.
Fixed-size, semantic, or hierarchical; overlap percentage; maximum chunk token size.
Dense-only, sparse-only, or hybrid; alpha weighting; query expansion technique.
Cross-encoder model choice or score-fusion-only fallback if latency budget is tight.
Maximum tokens reserved for retrieved context versus conversation history versus system prompt.
Which policy checks run pre- and post-generation; whether checks block or flag for human review.
Which metrics are measured; cadence of offline evaluation runs; threshold for regression alerts.

The enterprise RAG reference architecture divides document intelligence into two independently deployable pipelines. The indexing pipeline ingests, chunks, embeds, and indexes enterprise documents with their access control metadata. The query pipeline rewrites user queries, performs ACL-filtered hybrid retrieval, re-ranks candidates, assembles context, generates an answer with source citations, and applies guardrails before returning to the user.
ACL filtering must occur inside the vector store query, not in client-side post-processing.
Chunking and embedding model choices couple both pipelines — changing either requires a full re-index.
Hybrid retrieval (dense + sparse) outperforms either approach alone on enterprise knowledge bases with precise terminology.
Measure retrieval quality and generation quality separately; conflating them hides which pipeline is degrading.
Every architectural choice within the template must be documented in a project ADR and reviewed against the enterprise-approved technology list.
